episode_title: Education Freedom vs. Public Systems: Can We Do Both?
host: Dori Pham (UC Riverside School of Public Policy)
summary: This episode features Shaka Mitchell, a senior fellow at the American Federation for Children and constitutional law expert, discussing education reform and school choice policy. Mitchell argues that education choice (allowing families to use public funding across various educational settings including private, charter, and homeschools) can coexist with strong public school systems and is essential for serving diverse student needs, particularly students with disabilities. The discussion covers how constitutional law frameworks have evolved to support parental choice, the role of federal and state policies, and the importance of depolarizing education debates by building trust across ideological divides.
claims_to_note: Mitchell states that 1.2-1.3 million children participate in private choice programs nationally - this figure should be verified against current education statistics
Claims that 15% of K-12 students (~7.5 million) receive special education services under IDEA - census data should confirm this percentage

Asserts that Florida has approximately 1 million of 3 million school-age students in schools of choice (roughly 33%), stated as highest in country after Washington D.C. - enrollment figures should be verified

Claims that Ohio students exposed to more competition in school choice systems performed better, and public school students in competitive environments also improved - specific research studies should be identified to verify this claim
States that less than 5% of students in some schools can read on grade level - this appears to be anecdotal observation rather than specific verified data
policy_relevance: Federal Education Freedom Tax Credit established through major omnibus legislation (summer 2025) - allows tax credits for scholarship funds rather than direct government funding
Tennessee's Individualized Education Accounts (IEA) Program for students with special needs - portable funding that follows students to alternative educational settings
Supreme Court cases: Pierce v. Society of Sisters, Carson v. Macon, and Montana case Espinosa - establishing parental rights to direct child education including at religious institutions
Idaho Supreme Court decision (February 2026) upholding school choice program - interpreting state constitutional 'common schools' language as floor rather than ceiling
Wyoming school choice program litigation - currently blocked by injunction affecting scholarship distribution
Federal IDEA (Individuals with Disabilities Education Act) and ADA requirements for special education access
State constitutional provisions requiring 'systems of common schools' - discussed as framework for education policy
Proposed policy reform: Attorney fee-shifting for frivolous education lawsuits to reduce litigation barriers to school choice implementation
key_quotes: If we have an uneducated populace, I don't really have any great hope that we're going to make a lot of headway on, say, other economic challenges or climate change challenges or criminal recidivism or health care, right? If you have an uneducated populace, then almost necessarily lots of other public policy issues that come downstream from that are going to be hamstrung.

A child is not a creature of the state...a child, first and foremost, is the responsibility of a parent. The state government or local government or federal government doesn't get to tell a parent, to force a parent to educate their child in a certain manner and at a certain place.

I would have resources follow students to the setting that best meets their needs. And the why is because I really think that every person deserves to learn in a way that will help them access their fullest potential.
